mappings.rationaleAI: Bolivar's campaigns directly cover the struggle for independence in New Granada under Miranda and Bolivar (A.2.a.i), the expulsion of the Spanish from Venezuela, Colombia, Ecuador, and Panama (A.2.a.ii), San Martin's withdrawal and Bolivar's assumption of control for the final defeat of Spanish troops in Peru and Bolivia (A.2.c, A.2.d).
Washington's leadership in the US War of Independence influenced Latin American independence movements as part of the background forces including the US War of Independence (A.1.a).
Napoleon's seizure of the Spanish throne and the Peninsular War in Spain (1808–14) directly triggered Creole support for independence and the wars of liberation (A.1.c).

The Spanish Empire's colonial structure, Creole independence movements (1810s–1820s), and final losses directly map to the entire Latin American independence movement (A), the South American War of Independence (A.2), Mexico's war of independence (B), and Central American independence from Spain (C.1.a).
The American Revolution's influence as the US War of Independence is explicitly listed as a background factor for the Latin American independence movements (A.1.a).

Mexico's independence (1821), the Liberal reform era, and the 1910–20 Revolution comprehensively cover the Mexican War of Independence (A.3) and Mexico from independence through the end of the Revolution (B.1–4).

Toussaint-Louverture's slave revolt and Haitian independence (A.1.b), the growth of Cuba's sugar industry and abolition of slavery (C.2.c.i), and the cessation of the slave trade and abolition of slavery in Brazil (H.1.b) all connect to the Atlantic slave trade.
Central America's independence from Spain (1821), the federation of United Provinces, and individual statehood timelines directly cover the Central American republics (C.1, C.1.a).

South America's colonial history, independence movements, and nation-building cover the South American War of Independence (A.2), and the development of successor states of Gran Colombia (D), Andean nations (E), Chile (F), Rio de la Plata states (G), and Brazil (H).
British colonial and economic interests in Central America (C.1.h) and remaining European insular possessions in the Caribbean (C.2.d) connect to the British Empire's presence in the region.
Argentina's independence in 1816, 19th-century civil wars, confederation under Rosas, and national consolidation directly cover Argentina to 1930 (G.1.a–b).
Colombia's independence (1810), Gran Colombia, and the power struggle between Conservative and Liberal parties directly cover Colombia from 1819 to 1930 (D.2.a–b).

South American history from independence movements through 20th-century volatility broadly covers the independence wars (A.2) and subsequent national development across all South American sections (D–H).

Brazil's unique path to independence as an empire (1822) under Pedro I (A.2.e), the empire under Pedro II including abolition of slavery (H.1), and the First Republic (H.2) are comprehensively covered.
Enlightenment ideas of liberty and rational reform influenced Indian, Creole, and mestizo discontent as a background factor for the independence movement (A.1.a).
Spain's involvement in European wars and Napoleon's seizure of the throne (A.1.c), the South American independence wars against Spanish rule (A.2), and continued involvement in Mexican affairs (B.1).

European colonial domination and its dissolution through independence movements frame the entire section's treatment of Latin American independence (A), Mexican development (B), and Central American/Caribbean history (C).
US and European imperial interests including US protectorate over Cuba (C.2.c.iii), British colonial interests in Central America (C.1.h), and remaining European possessions in the Caribbean (C.2.d).
Mesoamerica's indigenous civilizations provide the pre-colonial context for Mexico's independence movement, with Hidalgo's revolt drawing on indigenous and mestizo discontent (A.3).
